text_blocks.text: In one experiment, Lavoisier observed that there
was no overall increase in weight when tin and air were
heated in a closed container. He noted that air
rushed in when he opened the container, which
indicated that part of the trapped air had been
consumed. He also noted that the tin had increased in
weight and that increase was the same as the weight
of the air that rushed back in. This and other
experiments on combustion were documented in his
book Sur la combustion en general, which was published
in 1777. In that work, he proved that air is a mixture of
two gases; 'vital air', which is essential to combustion
and respiration, and azote (Gk. azoton "lifeless"), which
did not support either. Azote later became nitrogen in
English, although it has kept the name in French and
